folio.label: 165v
folio.l:
pestilences æ ben in frendshipe , the grettest is flaterie , And æäfore it is
more nede , æ æè eschue & drede flat äeres , than any other peeple ,,

The
book seitú , æè shalt rathere , drede & flee , fro the swete wordes of
flatäyngö preysers , than fro the egre wordes of thy frend , æ seitú
the thyne sothes

¢ Salomou~ seitú , æ æe wordes of a flaterere ,
is a snare , to cacchen Innocentez , 72Cross in dry point in the right margin.ODR

he seitú also , æ he æ speketú
to his frend , wordes of swetnesse , & of plesaunce , settetú a nette
biforn~ his feet to cacchen hi~

¢ And æäfore seitú Tullyus ,, Encly-
ne nat thyne Eres to flatereres , ne take no cou~seil , of wordes of
flatäye

¢ And Catou~ seitú ¢ Auyse the wel , æè shalt eschue , wordes
of swetnesse & of plesance ,

And eke æè shalt eschue the cou~seilly~g
of thyne olde enemys , that ben reconsiled

¢ The book seitú , æat no
wigút retournetú safly , in to the gce , of his old enemy

¢ And
Ysope seitú , Ne trust nat to hem , to whicú æè hast had somtymeotiose dot on the y/ODR/
werre , or enemytee , ne telle hem nat thy cou~seil ,

And Seneca tel-
letú the cause why , It may nat be , seitú he , æ where as gret
fyr , hatú longe tyme endured , æ æäe ne dwelletú som vapour ,
of warmnesse ,,

And æäfore seitú Salamon ¢ In æin olde Foo , trost ne-
uere ,

for sikerly , thougú æin enemy be reconsiled , & makeæ æe chere
of humilitee , & loutetú to the , w his hed , ne trost hi~ neuäe ,

For cätes ,
he maketú thilke feyned humilitee , more for his ¸fyt , than for
any loue of thy ¹sone , by cause æ he demetú to haue victorie ouä æi
¹sone , by swicú feyned cou~tenance , æe whicú victorie , he mygút
nat haue by strif of werre

¢ And Peter Alfonce seitú , Make no
felaweshipe w thyne olde enemys , for if æè do he~ bounte , they wol
¹uäten it , in to wykkednesse , 72Cross in dry point in the right margin.ODR

And eke æè must eschue , the cou~seilly~gö
of he~ , æ ben æine säuantz , & beren the gret reuäence , for ¹auenture
they seyn it more for drede , æan for loue ,

And æäfore seitú a Phi-
losophre in this wyse , There is no wygút ¹fytly trewe , to hi~ , æ
he to sore dredetú

¢ And Tullius seitú , æäe nys no mygút so
gret of any Em¹our , æ longe may endure , but if he haue more
loue of the peeple , than drede

¢ æè shalt also eschue , æe cou~seilly~gö
of folk æ ben dronkelewe , for they ne can , no cou~seil hide

¢ For
Salamon seitú , æäe is no péuetee , ther as regnetú dronkenesse

¢ ©e
shuln also han in suspect , æe cou~seillyng of swicú folk , as cou~seille
©ow a thyng péuely , & cou~seille ©ow the contrie openly

¢ For Cassi-
dorie seitú , æ it is a manere sleigúte , to hyndree , whan he shew-
etú to don a thyng openly , & werk péuely the contrie

¢ Thow
shalt also haue in suspect , æe cou~seillyng of wykked folk , for here
cou~seil is alwey , ful of fraude

¢ And Dauid seitú , Blisful is æ
man , æ hatú nat folwed , the cou~seillyng of shrewes

¢ Thow shalt
also eschue , æe cou~seillyng of ©onge folk , for here cou~seil is nat
ripe

¢ Now sire , sithe I haue shewed ©ow , of whicú folk , ©e
shullen folwe æe cou~seil

¢ Now wol I teche ©ow , how ©e shuln
examynen ©oure cou~seil , after the docténe of Tullius
¢ In the
